Topic 5: The indictment & informant allegations

[143:41]Finally, the DOJ announced a major indictment against the Southern Poverty Law Center.

[143:47]And this indictment is sort of interesting because it reveals the true nature and the

[143:52]activities of the far left.

[143:54]So Southern Poverty Law Center is one of these legal activist organizations started up in

[144:00]the civil rights era.

[144:01]And it was supposed to be one of these groups that defends the rights of the minorities

[144:06]and the oppressed.

[144:08]They came up in the civil rights era and they were defending mostly black people from civil

[144:14]rights violations.

[144:15]But it was also Jews and all the other disaffected so-called marginalized groups.

[144:27]Because of a program that they have been running for almost 50 years,

[144:32]it's effectively a counterintelligence program.

[144:37]So you think of the SPLC and you think of legal activism.

[144:40]You think of how, for example, one of their activities,

[144:43]they have a program called Hate Watch.

[144:46]where they monitor hate organizations, hate speech, extremist groups. That's one of their

[144:52]activities. They also represent people in the event of a civil rights violation. I believe they

[144:57]brought some of the civil litigation against the Charlottesville organizers and they supported that.

[145:03]So these are some of the activities that they're known for and that you might imagine that they

[145:09]But this indictment, again, alleging fraud reveals that not only do they do all these

[145:15]ordinary things, but they also have been paying informants under the table to the tune of

[145:21]millions of dollars, upwards of $3 million to effectively go into far right extremist

[145:28]organizations, allegedly to spy on them.

[145:37]in the National Socialist Movement,

[145:40]people that attended and organized Charlottesville,

[145:44]all of the major so-called hate groups,

[145:47]neo-Nazi, white nationalist, Christian identity,

[145:51]groups like this,

[145:52]they have been paying people inside those organizations,

[145:55]in many cases, like lots of money,

[145:58]like a full-time salary, part-time or full-time salary.

[146:01]One individual has paid more than a million dollars.

[146:04]That's a lot of money.

[146:06]To be a deadbeat in the Klan or something and paying these people allegedly to spy on the activities of those

[146:13]organizations the DOJ says that that constitutes fraud because the SPLC is

[146:20]Representing to their donors that they're fighting extremism

[146:24]Hate groups hate speech when in reality no matter what you call it whether they're informants or whatever

[146:32]They are not being forthright about it.

[146:34]They're not being transparent about it.

[146:36]They are clandestinely and secretly giving money

[146:39]to those very same far-right organizations.

[146:42]And they're using what could be illegal accounting tricks

[146:45]to hide the money trail,

[146:47]to hide the paper trail for those disbursements.

[146:51]And this is the story.

[146:52]This is from the Associated Press.

[146:54]We'll just go through some of the details here.

[146:56]It says, quote,

[146:57]The Southern Poverty Law Center was indicted Tuesday on federal fraud charges alleging

[147:02]it had improperly raised millions of dollars to secretly pay leaders of the Ku Klux Klan

[147:07]and other hate groups for inside information.

[147:10]The Justice Department alleges a civil rights group defrauded donors by using their money

[147:15]to fund the very extremism acclaimed to be fighting with more than $3 million paid to

[147:20]informants through a now defunct program to infiltrate white supremacist and other extremist

[147:26]groups.

[147:27]The civil rights group faces charges of wire fraud, bank fraud, and conspiracy to commit

[147:31]money laundering in the case brought in the federal court in Alabama.

[147:35]The group said the program was used to monitor threats of violence and the information was often shared with local and federal law enforcement.

[147:43]The indictment includes details on at least nine unnamed informants paid by the SPLC through a secret program that prosecutors said began in the 1980s.

[147:53]Within the SPLC they were known as field sources or the F's according to the indictment.

[148:04]So a million dollars over nine years, that's like a six-figure salary practically. And that

[148:10]individual was affiliated with the Neo-Nazi National Alliance. Prosecutors say another

[148:17]informant was a member of the online leadership chat group that planned the 2017 Unite the Right

[148:25]The informant attended the rally at the direction of the SPLC and helped coordinate transportation

[148:30]for several others.

[148:31]That person was allegedly paid more than $270,000 between 2015 and 2023.

[148:38]So the first thing I want to say about this, and this is just, at this point, what even

[148:45]do you say?

[148:46]The first thing I want to do is clear the air.

[148:49]So this story comes out last week.

[148:53]And it's what I just read to you. The DOJ is indicting SPLC for fraud because of this informant program.

[148:59]They say it's defunct, but it's been operational since at least a few years ago.

[149:04]And basically the SPLC caught wind of this investigation.

[149:08]It started in the first Trump administration, shelved under Biden, came back in Trump too.

[149:15]And I believe the SPLC wound this program down

[149:18]because they caught wind that this was gonna be a problem

[149:22]for them.

[149:22]So it says it's defunct, but not really.

[193:23]States has imposed its own blockade over Iran.
